en.extract:
The tawny frogmouth (Podargus strigoides) is a species of frogmouth native to the Australian mainland and Tasmania and found throughout. It is a big-headed, stocky bird often mistaken for an owl due to its nocturnal habits and similar colouring.


Names
In the past, it was sometimes mistakenly called a mopoke or mopawk, a name used for the Australian boobook, the call of which is often confused with that of the tawny frogmouth.


Taxonomy
The tawny frogmouth was first described in 1801 by English naturalist John Latham. Its specific epithet is derived from Latin strix "owl" and oides "form". Tawny frogmouths belong to the frogmouth genus Podargus, which includes the two other species of frogmouths found within Australia, the marbled frogmouth and the Papuan frogmouth. The frogmouths form a well-defined group within the order Caprimulgiformes. Although related to owls, their closest relatives are the oilbirds, potoos, owlet-nightjars, and true nightjars. The earliest fossil evidence of frogmouths, from the Eocene, implies that they diverged from their closest relatives during the early Tertiary. Three subspecies of the tawny frogmouth are currently recognised:

P. s. phalaenoides is found throughout Northern Australia southwards to the Great Sandy Desert, Barkly Tableland, and the Gulf of Carpentaria in Queensland.
P. s. brachypterus is found in Western Australia northward to the Great Sandy Desert, north-eastward to the Channel Country of Queensland, and south-eastward to the Murray Mallee in Victoria.
P. s. strigoides is found in eastern and south-eastern Australia from north of Cooktown, westward to the inland fringes of the Great Dividing Range, and in Tasmania.


Description

Tawny frogmouths are large, big-headed birds that can measure from 34 to 53 cm (13 to 21 in) long. Weights have been recorded up to 680 g (1.50 lb) in the wild (and perhaps even more in captivity), but these are exceptionally high. In the nominate race, 55 males were found to weigh a mean of 354 g (12.5 oz), while 39 females weighed a mean of 297 g (10.5 oz), with a range between both of 157 to 555 g (5.5 to 19.6 oz). Among the subspecies P. s. brachypterus, 20 unsexed birds were found to average 278 g (9.8 oz) with a range of 185 to 416 g (6.5 to 14.7 oz). In P. s. phalaenoides, a weight range of 205 to 364 g (7.2 to 12.8 oz) was reported. Thus, in terms of average if not maximal body mass, the tawny is a bit smaller than its relative, the Papuan frogmouth. On average, the life expectancy of wild tawny frogmouths is up to 14 years and for those in captivity, as high as 30+ years. Tawny frogmouths are stocky and compact with rounded wings and short legs. They have wide, heavy, olive-grey to blackish bills that are hooked at the tip and topped with distinctive tufts of bristles. Their eyes are large and yellow, a trait shared by owls. However, they are not forward facing like an owl's.
Tawny frogmouths have three distinct colour morphs, grey being the most common in both sexes. Males of this morph have silver-grey upperparts with black streaks and slightly paler underparts with white barring and brown to rufous mottling. Females of this morph are often darker with more rufous mottling. Females of the subspecies P. s. strigoides have a chestnut morph and females of the subspecies P. s. phalaenoides have a rufous morph. Leucistic or albinistic all-white aberrant plumage for this species has been documented.


Camouflage

The tawny frogmouth makes use of cryptic plumage and mimicry to camouflage itself. These birds strategically perch themselves on low tree branches during daylight hours, cleverly assimilating with the tree itself.  Their silvery-grey plumage, adorned with patterns of white, black, and brown streaks and mottles, enables them to seamlessly blend into the appearance of a fractured tree branch, rendering them nearly invisible in the bright light of day.
In a display of adaptation, the tawny frogmouth typically selects a segment of a tree branch that displays signs of breakage, perching upon it with its head elegantly inclined upwards at a distinctive angle. This posturing, coupled with their large and broad beaks, contributes to the resemblance they achieve. Frequently, a pair of tawny frogmouths will position themselves side by side, simultaneously angling their heads upwards. Only when closely approached do they emerge from their concealed positions, either taking flight or issuing warning signals to potential predators.
When faced with threats, adult tawny frogmouths employ a distinctive alarm call that serves as a signal to their chicks. This call instructs the young birds to maintain a state of silence and immobility, ensuring that their natural plumage camouflage remains intact and uncompromised.


Differences from owls
Tawny frogmouths and owls both have mottled patterns, wide eyes, and anisodactyl feet. However, owls are birds of prey that possess strong legs, powerful talons, and toes with a unique flexible joint they use to catch prey. Tawny frogmouths are insectivores who prefer to catch their prey with their beaks and have fairly weak feet. They roost out in the open, relying on camouflage for defence, and build their nests in tree forks, whereas owls roost hidden in thick foliage and build their nests in tree hollows. Tawny frogmouths have wide, forward-facing beaks for catching insects, whereas owls have narrow, downwards-facing beaks used to tear prey apart. The eyes of tawny frogmouths are to the side of the face, while the eyes of owls are fully forward on the face. Furthermore, owls have full or partial face discs and large, asymmetrical ears, while tawny frogmouths do not.


Distribution and habitat
Tawny frogmouths are found throughout most of the Australian mainland except in far western Queensland, the central Northern Territory, and most of the Nullarbor Plain. In Tasmania, they are common throughout the northern and eastern parts of the state.
They can be found in almost any habitat type, including forests and woodlands, scrub and heathland vegetation, and savannahs, but they are rarely seen in heavy rainforests and treeless deserts. They are seen in large numbers in areas populated with many river gums and casuarinas and can be found along river courses if these areas are timbered. Tawny frogmouths are a common nocturnal urban wildlife, especially in residential suburbs, having adapted to human presence. They have been reported nesting in parks and gardens with trees.


Behaviour and ecology


Diet and feeding

Tawny frogmouths are carnivorous and are considered to be among Australia's most effective pest-control birds, as their diet consists largely of species regarded as vermin or pests in houses, farms, and gardens. The bulk of their diet is composed of large nocturnal insects, such as moths, as well as spiders, worms, slugs, and snails, but also includes a variety of bugs, beetles, wasps, ants, centipedes, millipedes, and scorpions. Large numbers of invertebrates are consumed to make up sufficient biomass. Small mammals, reptiles, frogs, and birds are also eaten.
During daylight hours, healthy tawny frogmouths generally do not actively look for food, though they may sit with their mouths open, snapping them shut when an insect enters. As dusk approaches, they begin actively searching for food. They feed mainly by pouncing from a tree or other elevated perch to take large insects or small vertebrates from the ground using their beaks with great precision. Some smaller prey, such as moths, can be caught in flight. Foraging flights consist of short, snatching flights to foliage, branches, or into the air.
Tawny frogmouths do not consume prey collected on the ground or in flight on the spot unless it is very small. The captured prey is held in the tip of the beak and taken to a nearby branch, where it is then processed. Insects are generally pulped at the rim of the beak before being swallowed, and larger prey such as lizards or mice are generally killed before consumption by being vigorously struck against a branch.


Bonding and breeding
Tawny frogmouths form partnerships for life, and once established, pairs usually stay in the same territory for a decade or more. Establishing and maintaining physical contact is an integral part of their lifelong bond. During breeding season, pairs roost closely together on the same branch, often with their bodies touching. The male carries out grooming by gently stroking through the plumage of the female with his beak in sessions that can last for 10 minutes or more.
The breeding season of tawny frogmouths is from August to December, but individuals in arid areas are known to breed in response to heavy rains. Males and females both share in the building of nests by collecting twigs and mouthfuls of leaves and dropping them into position. Nests are usually placed on horizontal, forked tree branches and can reach up to 30 cm (12 in) in diameter. Loose sticks are piled together, and leaf litter and grass stems are placed to soften the centre. The nests are very fragile and can disintegrate easily.
The clutch size of the tawny frogmouth is one to three eggs. Both sexes share incubation of the eggs during the night, whilst during the day, males incubate the eggs. For the duration of the incubation period, the nest is rarely left unattended. One partner roosts on a nearby branch and provides food for the brooding partner. Once hatched, both parents cooperate in the supply of food to the young. The fledging period of the tawny frogmouth is 25 to 35 days, during which they develop half their adult mass.


Vocalisations
Tawny frogmouths have a wide range of vocalisations that can signal information about sex, territory, food, or predators. They generally use low-amplitude and low-frequency sounds to communicate, though some of their warning screams can be heard for miles. Nestlings make a number of unique calls expressing distress, hunger, or fear. Juveniles retain this range while developing a loud call for begging. Nestlings, juveniles, and adults all use a low-amplitude annoyance call meant for family members. When disturbed during rest, they can emit a soft warning buzz that sounds similar to a bee, and when threatened, they can make a loud hissing noise and produce clacking sounds with their beaks.
At night, tawny frogmouths emit a deep and continuous "oom-oom-oom" grunting at a frequency of about eight calls in 5 seconds. The steady grunts are often repeated a number of times throughout the night. They also make a soft, breathy "whoo-whoo-whoo" call at night of lower intensity, but at the same frequency. Before and during breeding season, males and females perform duets consisting of call sequences that either alternate between partners or are performed simultaneously. Tawny frogmouths also make distinctive drumming noises during breeding season.


Thermoregulation

The wide distribution range of the tawny frogmouth includes areas of the Australian continent where winter night temperatures regularly approach or grow colder than 0 °C (32 °F) and warm summers can have extremes above 40 °C (104 °F). The high temperatures in summer and low temperatures in winter provide a thermoregulatory challenge for tawny frogmouths that roost all day out in the open.
Significant differences in the orientation of tawny frogmouths on branches have been observed during winter and summer. During summer when light intensity is at maximum strength, they tend to choose positions on branches that do not have all-day exposure to sunlight. Physiological testing has shown that they are able to triple their breathing rate without the need to open their beaks. However, when their body temperature rises by as much as 4–5°C, they begin to pant. Faced with further heat stress, tawny frogmouths engorge the blood vessels in the mouth to increase the flow of blood to the buccal area and produce a mucus that helps to cool air as it is inhaled, hence cooling the body.
During winter, tawny frogmouths choose northerly oriented positions on branches that are more exposed to sunlight to increase body heat. Pair roosting and huddling to share body warmth is also common during winter. During daylight, tawny frogmouths sometimes perch on the ground to sunbathe, remaining motionless up to 5 minutes. During this time, the birds open their beaks wide, close their eyes, and move their heads to the side to allow sunrays to penetrate beneath the thick layer of feathers.


Torpor
During winter, the food supply shrinks drastically and prewinter body fat stores can only provide a limited supply of energy. Tawny frogmouths are unable to survive the winter months without spending much of their days and nights in torpor. Torpor results in energy conservation by significantly slowing down heart rate and metabolism, which lowers body temperature. Torpor is different from hibernation in that it only lasts for relatively short periods of time, usually a few hours. Shallow torpor lasts for several hours and is a regular, daily occurrence in the winter. Dawn torpor bouts are shorter and temperature reduction may be as small as 0.5 to 1.5 °C, while night torpor bouts last several hours and can reduce body temperature by up to 10 °C.


Conservation and threats

The conservation status of tawny frogmouths is "least concern" due to their widespread distribution. A number of ongoing threats to the health of the population, though, are known. Many bird and mammalian carnivores are known to prey upon the tawny frogmouth. Native birds, including ravens, butcherbirds, and currawongs, may steal the protein-rich eggs to feed their own young. Birds of prey such as hobbies and falcons, as well as rodents and tree-climbing snakes, also cause major damage to the clutches by taking eggs and nestlings. In subtropical areas where food is available throughout the year, tawny frogmouths sometimes start brooding earlier in winter to avoid the awakening of snakes after brumation. Since 1998, a cluster of cases of neurological disease has occurred in tawny frogmouths in the Sydney area, caused by the parasite Angiostrongylus cantonensis, a rat lungworm.


Human impact
Tawny frogmouths face a number of threats from human activities and pets. They are often killed or injured on rural roads during feeding, as they fly in front of cars when chasing insects illuminated in the beam of the headlights. Large-scale land clearing of eucalypt trees and intense bushfires are serious threats to their populations, as they tend not to move to other areas if their homes are destroyed. House cats are the most significant introduced predator of the tawny frogmouth, but dogs and foxes are known to also occasionally kill the birds. When tawny frogmouths pounce to catch prey on the ground, they are slow to return to flight and vulnerable to attack from these predators.
As they have adapted to live in close proximity to human populations, tawny frogmouths are at high risk of exposure to pesticides. Continued widespread use of insecticides and rodent poisons are hazardous, as they remain in the system of the target animal and can be fatal to a tawny frogmouth that eats them. The effect of these toxins is often indirect, as they can be absorbed into fatty tissue with the bird experiencing no overt signs of ill health until the winter, when the fat deposits are drawn on and the poison enters the bloodstream.


References


External links

Australian museum online
National Parks and Wildlife Service
Tawny frogmouth videos, photos & sounds on the Internet Bird Collection
Tawny frogmouth videos Archived 2011-06-04 at the Wayback Machine on Google Video
Tawny frogmouth videos on YouTube

The '''tawny frogmouth''' ('''''Podargus strigoides''''') is a species of [[frogmouth]] native to the Australian mainland and [[Tasmania]] and found throughout. It is a big-headed, stocky bird often mistaken for an [[owl]] due to its [[nocturnal]] habits and similar colouring.<ref name="Owl Pages"/>

==Names==
In the past, it was sometimes mistakenly called a ''mopoke'' or ''mopawk'',<ref>{{cite book |last=Lumholtz |first=Carl |url=https://archive.org/details/amongcannibalsac1889lumh/page/n33/mode/2up |title=Among cannibals; an account of four years' travels in Australia and of camp life with the aborigines of Queensland |date=1889 |page=33 |location=New York |publisher=C. Scribner's sons |author-link=Carl Sofus Lumholtz}}</ref> a name used for the [[Australian boobook]], the call of which is often confused with that of the tawny frogmouth.

==Taxonomy==
The tawny frogmouth was first described in 1801 by English naturalist [[John Latham (ornithologist)|John Latham]].<ref name=FMPA>{{cite web|title=Husbandry Guidelines for Tawny Frogmouth|url=http://nswfmpa.org/Husbandry%20Manuals/Published%20Manuals/Aves/Tawny%20Frogmouth.pdf|publisher=NSW Fauna & Marine Parks Association Inc.|access-date=2 June 2014}}</ref> Its [[specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]] is derived from [[Latin]] {{Lang|la|strix}} "owl" and {{Lang|la|oides}} "form". Tawny frogmouths belong to the frogmouth genus ''[[Podargus]]'', which includes the two other species of frogmouths found within Australia, the [[marbled frogmouth]] and the [[Papuan frogmouth]].<ref name=Kaplan/> The frogmouths form a well-defined group within the order [[Caprimulgiformes]].<ref name=Grzimek/> Although related to owls, their closest relatives are the [[oilbird]]s, [[potoo]]s, [[owlet-nightjar]]s, and true [[nightjar]]s.<ref name="Kaplan"/> The earliest fossil evidence of frogmouths, from the [[Eocene]], implies that they diverged from their closest relatives during the early [[Tertiary period|Tertiary]].<ref name=Grzimek/> Three [[subspecies]] of the tawny frogmouth are currently recognised:
* ''P. s. phalaenoides'' is found throughout [[Northern Australia]] southwards to the [[Great Sandy Desert]], [[Barkly Tableland]], and the [[Gulf of Carpentaria]] in [[Queensland]].<ref name=IBC>{{cite web|title=Tawny Frogmouth (Podargus strigoides)|url=http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/tawny-frogmouth-podargus-strigoides|publisher=Internet Bird Collection|access-date=2 June 2014}}</ref>
* ''P. s. brachypterus'' is found in [[Western Australia]] northward to the Great Sandy Desert, north-eastward to the [[Channel Country]] of Queensland, and south-eastward to the [[Murray Mallee]] in [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]].<ref name=IBC/>
* ''P. s. strigoides'' is found in eastern and south-eastern Australia from north of [[Cooktown]], westward to the inland fringes of the [[Great Dividing Range]], and in Tasmania.<ref name=IBC/>

==Description==
[[File:Tawny frogmouth wholebody444.jpg|thumb|left|Rufous morph]]
Tawny frogmouths are large, big-headed birds that can measure from {{cvt|34|to|53|cm}} long. Weights have been recorded up to {{cvt|680|g|lb}} in the wild (and perhaps even more in captivity), but these are exceptionally high.<ref name=Grzimek/><ref>{{cite book| last= Holyoak| first= David| year= 2001| title= Nightjars and Their Allies| publisher= Oxford University Press |isbn= 978-0-19-854987-1}}</ref><ref name="CRC">{{cite book| title= CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses| edition= 2nd |editor-first= John B. Jr.| editor-last=  Dunning| publisher= CRC Press | year= 2008 |isbn= 978-1-4200-6444-5}}</ref> In the nominate race, 55 males were found to weigh a mean of {{cvt|354|g}}, while 39 females weighed a mean of {{cvt|297|g}}, with a range between both of {{cvt|157|to|555|g}}. Among the subspecies ''P. s. brachypterus'', 20 unsexed birds were found to average {{cvt|278|g}} with a range of {{cvt|185|to|416|g}}.<ref name="CRC"/> In ''P. s. phalaenoides'', a weight range of {{cvt|205|to|364|g}} was reported.<ref>{{cite book| last1= Del Hoyo| first1= J.| last2= Elliot| first2= A.| last3= Sargatal| first3= J. |year= 1992| title= Handbook of the Birds of the World| place= Barcelona| publisher= Lynx Editions| isbn= }}</ref> Thus, in terms of average if not maximal body mass, the tawny is a bit smaller than its relative, the Papuan frogmouth.<ref name="CRC"/> On average, the life expectancy of wild tawny frogmouths is up to 14 years and for those in captivity, as high as 30+ years.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Tawny-Frogmouth |url= https://www.idahofallsidaho.gov/DocumentCenter/View/4980/Tawny-Frogmouth |website= idahofallsidaho.gov| publisher= [[Idaho Falls, Idaho]] |format=PDF| date= |url-status=live |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20240217142352/https://www.idahofallsidaho.gov/DocumentCenter/View/4980/Tawny-Frogmouth |archive-date=17 February 2024 |access-date=19 June 2024}}</ref> Tawny frogmouths are stocky and compact with rounded wings and short legs. They have wide, heavy, olive-grey to blackish bills that are hooked at the tip and topped with distinctive tufts of bristles.<ref name= Parks&Wildlife>{{cite web | title= Tawny Frogmouth, Podargus Strigoides | url= http://www.parks.tas.gov.au/indeX.aspX?base=24031 | website= parks.tas.gov.au | publisher= Parks & Wildlife Service | place= Australia | access-date= 2 May 2014 | archive-date= 25 September 2019 | archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20190925125819/https://www.parks.tas.gov.au/index.aspx?base=24031 }}</ref> Their eyes are large and yellow, a trait shared by owls.<ref name="Kaplan">{{cite book |last=Kaplan |first= Gisela |author-link= Gisela Kaplan |title= Tawny Frogmouth |date=2007 |publisher= CSIRO Publishing |location=Collingwood, Victoria |isbn=978-0-643-09509-0}}</ref> However, they are not forward facing like an owl's.<ref name= "FMPA"/>

Tawny frogmouths have three distinct colour [[Polymorphism (biology)|morphs]], grey being the most common in both sexes.<ref name= Cleere>{{cite journal|last=Cleere|first=N.|title=Aberrant plumage in the Tawny Frogmouth, Podargus strigoides| journal= [[Emu (journal)|Emu]] | year= 2002 |volume= 102 |issue= 2 |page=195|doi=10.1071/mu01015|bibcode=2002EmuAO.102..195C |s2cid=86375502}}</ref> Males of this morph have silver-grey upperparts with black streaks and slightly paler underparts with white barring and brown to [[rufous]] mottling. Females of this morph are often darker with more rufous mottling.<ref name=Grzimek>{{cite book|last=Grzimek|first=Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Grzimek|title=Grzimek's Animal Life Encyclopedia|date=2003|publisher=Thomson-Gale|isbn=978-0-7876-5362-0}}</ref> Females of the subspecies ''P. s. strigoides'' have a chestnut morph and females of the subspecies ''P. s. phalaenoides'' have a rufous morph.<ref name= Cleere/> [[Leucistic]] or [[albinistic]] all-white aberrant [[plumage]] for this species has been documented.<ref name= Cleere/>

===Camouflage===
[[File:Tawny Frogmouth (Coverdale).jpg|thumb|left|upright|Camouflaged tawny frogmouths blend in with colour and texture of tree bark in Sydney.]]
[[File:TawnyFrogmouthBirdsInAfternoonSun.jpg|thumb|right|Camouflaged tawny frogmouth couple in afternoon sun, Melbourne]]
The tawny frogmouth makes use of [[crypsis|cryptic plumage]] and [[mimicry]] to camouflage itself. These birds strategically perch themselves on low tree branches during daylight hours, cleverly assimilating with the tree itself.<ref name=Thomson>{{cite journal| last= Thomson|first=D.|title=Notes on the Tawny Frogmouth (Podargus strigoides)| journal= Emu |year= 1922 |volume= 22 |issue= 4 |page= 307 |doi= 10.1071/mu922307|url=https://zenodo.org/record/1429904|doi-access= free}}</ref>  Their silvery-grey plumage, adorned with patterns of white, black, and brown streaks and mottles,<ref name=Olsen>{{cite book| last= Olsen |first=Penny|author-link=Penny Olsen| title=Stray Feathers: Reflections on the Structure, Behaviour and Evolution of Birds |year= 2011|publisher=CSIRO Publishing| location= Collingwood, Victoria |isbn=978-0-643-10344-3}}</ref> enables them to seamlessly blend into the appearance of a fractured tree branch, rendering them nearly invisible in the bright light of day.<ref name="LPZTawnyFrogmouth">{{cite web| url= http://www.lpzoo.org/animals/factsheet/tawny-frogmouth |title= Tawny Frogmouth Fact Sheet| website= lpzoo.org| location= Chicago| publisher= [[Lincoln Park Zoo]] | date= | archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20120807012602/https://www.lpzoo.org/animals/factsheet/tawny-frogmouth| archive-date= 2012-08-07| access-date= 2025-07-23}}</ref><ref name=ReptilePark>{{cite web|title=Tawny Frogmouth – Podargus Strigoides |url= http://www.reptilepark.com.au/animalprofile.asp?id=31| website= reptilepark.com.au| place= Australia |publisher=[[Australian Reptile Park]] |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20150627161154/http://www.reptilepark.com.au/animalprofile.asp?id=31 |archive-date=27 June 2015 |access-date=2 May 2014}}</ref>

In a display of adaptation, the tawny frogmouth typically selects a segment of a tree branch that displays signs of breakage, perching upon it with its head elegantly inclined upwards at a distinctive angle. This posturing, coupled with their large and broad beaks, contributes to the resemblance they achieve.<ref name="Thomson" /> Frequently, a pair of tawny frogmouths will position themselves side by side, simultaneously angling their heads upwards. Only when closely approached do they emerge from their concealed positions, either taking flight or issuing warning signals to potential predators.<ref name="ReptilePark" />

When faced with threats, adult tawny frogmouths employ a distinctive [[alarm signal|alarm call]] that serves as a signal to their chicks. This call instructs the young birds to maintain a state of silence and immobility, ensuring that their natural plumage camouflage remains intact and uncompromised.<ref name="Olsen" />

[[File:Podargus strigoides - Family of four - camouflage position.jpg|thumb|Family of four in alarm position, emulating broken branches]]

===Differences from owls===
Tawny frogmouths and owls both have mottled patterns, wide eyes, and [[anisodactyl]] feet. However, owls are [[birds of prey]] that possess strong legs, powerful [[talons]], and toes with a unique flexible joint they use to catch prey.<ref name="Owl Pages">{{cite web| title= Myth of the Tawny Frogmouth "Owl"|url=http://www.owlpages.com/articles.php?section=Other+Owl+Stuff&title=Tawny+Frogmouth| website= owlpages.com| publisher= The Owl Pages|access-date=2 June 2014}}</ref> Tawny frogmouths are [[insectivore]]s who prefer to catch their prey with their beaks and have fairly weak feet.<ref name=FMPA/> They roost out in the open, relying on camouflage for defence, and build their nests in tree forks, whereas owls roost hidden in thick foliage and build their nests in tree hollows.<ref name="Owl Pages"/> Tawny frogmouths have wide, forward-facing beaks for catching insects, whereas owls have narrow, downwards-facing beaks used to tear prey apart.<ref name="Owl Pages"/> The eyes of tawny frogmouths are to the side of the face, while the eyes of owls are fully forward on the face.<ref name=FMPA/> Furthermore, owls have full or partial [[facial disc|face discs]] and large, [[asymmetrical]] ears, while tawny frogmouths do not.<ref name=FMPA/>

==Distribution and habitat==
Tawny frogmouths are found throughout most of the Australian mainland<ref name=ReptilePark/> except in far western [[Queensland]], the central [[Northern Territory]], and most of the [[Nullarbor Plain]].<ref name=Kaplan/> In [[Tasmania]], they are common throughout the northern and eastern parts of the state.<ref name=Parks&Wildlife/>

They can be found in almost any habitat type, including forests and [[woodland]]s, [[shrubland|scrub]] and [[heath]]land vegetation, and [[savannah]]s,<ref name=AusMuseum>{{cite web|title=Tawny Frogmouth|url=http://australianmuseum.net.au/tawny-frogmouth|publisher=[[Australian Museum]]|access-date=2 May 2014}}</ref> but they are rarely seen in heavy rainforests and treeless deserts.<ref name=PerthZoo>{{cite web|title=Tawny Frogmouth|url=http://www.perthzoo.wa.gov.au/animals-plants/australia/nocturnal-house/tawny-frogmouth|publisher=[[Perth Zoo]]|access-date=2 May 2014|archive-date=29 November 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161129215310/http://perthzoo.wa.gov.au/animals-plants/australia/nocturnal-house/tawny-frogmouth/}}</ref> They are seen in large numbers in areas populated with many river gums and [[casuarina]]s and can be found along river courses if these areas are timbered.<ref name=Kaplan/> Tawny frogmouths are a common nocturnal [[urban wildlife]], especially in residential suburbs, having adapted to human presence. They have been reported nesting in parks and gardens with trees.<ref name=Kaplan/>

==Behaviour and ecology==

===Diet and feeding===
[[File:Tawny Frogmouth Skull.jpg|thumb|left|Tawny frogmouth skull]]
Tawny frogmouths are [[carnivorous]]<ref name=PerthZoo/> and are considered to be among Australia's most effective pest-control birds, as their diet consists largely of species regarded as [[vermin]] or [[pest (organism)|pests]] in houses, farms, and gardens.<ref name=Kaplan/> The bulk of their diet is composed of large [[nocturnal]] insects, such as [[moth]]s, as well as [[spider]]s, [[worm]]s, [[slug]]s, and [[snail]]s,<ref name=Parks&Wildlife/> but also includes a variety of [[hemiptera|bugs]], [[beetle]]s, [[wasp]]s, [[ant]]s, [[centipede]]s, [[millipede]]s, and [[scorpion]]s.<ref name=Kaplan/> Large numbers of [[invertebrate]]s are consumed to make up sufficient [[biomass]].<ref name=Kaplan/> Small [[mammal]]s, [[reptile]]s, [[frog]]s, and birds are also eaten.<ref name=AusMuseum/>

During daylight hours, healthy tawny frogmouths generally do not actively look for food, though they may sit with their mouths open, snapping them shut when an insect enters.<ref name=Olsen/> As dusk approaches, they begin actively searching for food. They feed mainly by pouncing from a tree or other elevated perch to take large insects or small [[vertebrate]]s from the ground<ref name=Grzimek/> using their beaks with great precision. Some smaller prey, such as moths, can be caught in flight.<ref name=AusMuseum/> Foraging flights consist of short, snatching flights to foliage, branches, or into the air.<ref name=Kaplan/>

Tawny frogmouths do not consume prey collected on the ground or in flight on the spot unless it is very small.<ref name=Kaplan/> The captured prey is held in the tip of the beak and taken to a nearby branch, where it is then processed. Insects are generally pulped at the rim of the beak before being swallowed, and larger prey such as [[lizard]]s or [[mice]] are generally killed before consumption by being vigorously struck against a branch.<ref name=Kaplan/>

===Bonding and breeding===
Tawny frogmouths form partnerships for life, and once established, pairs usually stay in the same territory for a decade or more.<ref name=Kaplan/> Establishing and maintaining physical contact is an integral part of their lifelong bond. During [[breeding season]], pairs roost closely together on the same branch, often with their bodies touching. The male carries out grooming by gently stroking through the plumage of the female with his beak in sessions that can last for 10 minutes or more.<ref name=Kaplan/>

The breeding season of tawny frogmouths is from August to December, but individuals in arid areas are known to breed in response to heavy rains.<ref name=AusMuseum/> Males and females both share in the building of [[nest]]s by collecting twigs and mouthfuls of leaves and dropping them into position.<ref name=Kaplan/> Nests are usually placed on horizontal, forked tree branches and can reach up to {{Convert abbreviated|30|cm}} in diameter.<ref name=ReptilePark/> Loose sticks are piled together, and [[leaf litter]] and grass stems are placed to soften the centre. The nests are very fragile and can disintegrate easily.<ref name=Kaplan/>

The [[clutch (eggs)|clutch]] size of the tawny frogmouth is one to three eggs.<ref name=Grzimek/> Both sexes share [[egg incubation|incubation]] of the eggs during the night, whilst during the day, males incubate the eggs.<ref name=Kortner>{{cite journal|last=Kortner|first=G.|author2=Geiser, F. |title=Nesting behaviour and juvenile development of the Tawny Frogmouth|journal=Emu|date=1999|volume=99|issue=3|pages=212–217|doi=10.1071/mu99024 }}</ref> For the duration of the incubation period, the nest is rarely left unattended. One partner roosts on a nearby branch and provides food for the brooding partner.<ref name=Kaplan/> Once hatched, both parents cooperate in the supply of food to the young.<ref name=ReptilePark/> The [[fledge|fledging]] period of the tawny frogmouth is 25 to 35 days, during which they develop half their adult mass.<ref name=Kortner/>

===Vocalisations===
Tawny frogmouths have a wide range of [[Bird vocalization|vocalisations]] that can signal information about sex, territory, food, or predators. They generally use low-[[amplitude]] and low-[[frequency]] sounds to communicate, though some of their warning screams can be heard for miles.<ref name=Kaplan/> [[Nestlings]] make a number of unique calls expressing distress, hunger, or fear. [[Juvenile (organism)|Juveniles]] retain this range while developing a loud call for begging. Nestlings, juveniles, and adults all use a low-amplitude annoyance call meant for family members.<ref name=Kaplan/> When disturbed during rest, they can emit a soft warning buzz that sounds similar to a [[bee]], and when threatened, they can make a loud hissing noise and produce clacking sounds with their beaks.<ref name=Higgins>{{cite book|last=Higgins|first=Peter|title=Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds, Volume 4|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=Melbourne|isbn=978-0-19-553244-9|year=2006}}</ref>

At night, tawny frogmouths emit a deep and continuous "oom-oom-oom" grunting<ref name=Higgins/> at a frequency of about eight calls in 5 seconds.<ref name=Hoyo>{{cite book|last=Hoyo|first=Josep del|title=Handbook of the Birds of the World, Volume 5|year=1992 |publisher=Lynx Editions|location=Barcelona|isbn=978-84-87334-25-2|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0001unse}}</ref> The steady grunts are often repeated a number of times throughout the night. They also make a soft, breathy "whoo-whoo-whoo" call at night of lower intensity, but at the same frequency.<ref name=Kaplan/> Before and during breeding season, males and females perform [[duet]]s consisting of call sequences that either alternate between partners or are performed simultaneously.<ref name=Kaplan/> Tawny frogmouths also make distinctive drumming noises during breeding season.<ref name=Hoyo/>

===Thermoregulation===
[[File:Tawny Frogmouth (Podargus strigoides) 2.jpg|thumb|right|upright|Pair of tawny frogmouths in Brisbane Botanical Gardens]]

The wide distribution range of the tawny frogmouth includes areas of the Australian continent where winter night temperatures regularly approach or grow colder than {{Convert|0|C}} and warm summers can have extremes above {{Convert|40|C}}.<ref name=Geiser>{{cite journal|last=Kortner|first=G.|author2=Geiser, F. |title=Roosting behaviour of the Tawny Frogmouth (''Podargus strigoides'') |journal=Journal of Zoology|date=1999|volume=248|issue=4|pages=501–507 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-7998.1999.tb01049.x |url= }}</ref> The high temperatures in summer and low temperatures in winter provide a [[thermoregulatory]] challenge for tawny frogmouths that roost all day out in the open.

Significant differences in the orientation of tawny frogmouths on branches have been observed during winter and summer.<ref name=Kaplan/> During summer when light intensity is at maximum strength, they tend to choose positions on branches that do not have all-day exposure to sunlight. [[Physiological]] testing has shown that they are able to triple their breathing rate without the need to open their beaks.<ref name=Kaplan/> However, when their body temperature rises by as much as 4–5°C, they begin to pant.<ref name="Bech">{{cite journal|last=Bech|first=C.|author2=Nicol, S. |title=. Thermoregulation and ventilation in the Tawny Frogmouth, ''Podargus strigoides'': a low-metabolic avian species|journal=Australian Journal of Zoology|date=1999|volume=47|issue=2|pages=143–153|doi=10.1071/zo98058}}</ref> Faced with further heat stress, tawny frogmouths engorge the [[blood vessel]]s in the mouth to increase the flow of blood to the [[buccal space|buccal]] area and produce a [[mucus]] that helps to cool air as it is inhaled, hence cooling the body.<ref name=Lasiewski>{{cite journal|last=Lasiewski|first=R.|author2=Bartholemew, G. |title=Evaporative cooling in the Poor-Will and the Tawny Frogmouth|journal=The Condor|date=1966|volume=68|issue=3|pages=253–262|doi=10.2307/1365559|jstor=1365559}}</ref>

During winter, tawny frogmouths choose northerly oriented positions on branches that are more exposed to sunlight to increase body heat.<ref name=Kaplan/> Pair roosting and huddling to share body warmth is also common during winter.<ref name=Geiser/> During daylight, tawny frogmouths sometimes perch on the ground to sunbathe, remaining motionless up to 5 minutes. During this time, the birds open their beaks wide, close their eyes, and move their heads to the side to allow sunrays to penetrate beneath the thick layer of feathers.<ref name=Kaplan/>

===Torpor===
During winter, the food supply shrinks drastically and prewinter body fat stores can only provide a limited supply of energy.<ref name=Brigham>{{cite journal|last=Kortner|first=G.|title=Torpor in free-ranging Tawny Frogmouths (''Podargus strigoides'') |journal=Physiological and Biochemical Zoology|date=2001|volume=74|issue=6|pages=789–797|doi=10.1086/324097 |pmid=11731971 |s2cid=14302926 |display-authors=etal |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/11624162 }}</ref> Tawny frogmouths are unable to survive the winter months without spending much of their days and nights in [[torpor]].<ref name=BackyardBuddies>{{cite web|title=Tawny Frogmouths|url=http://backyardbuddies.net.au/birds/insect-eaters/tawny-frogmouths|publisher=Backyard Buddies|access-date=20 May 2014|archive-date=29 February 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160229114559/http://backyardbuddies.net.au/birds/insect-eaters/tawny-frogmouths}}</ref> Torpor results in energy conservation by significantly slowing down [[heart rate]] and [[metabolism]], which lowers body temperature.<ref name=Brigham/> Torpor is different from [[hibernation]] in that it only lasts for relatively short periods of time, usually a few hours. Shallow torpor lasts for several hours and is a regular, daily occurrence in the winter. Dawn torpor bouts are shorter and temperature reduction may be as small as 0.5 to 1.5 °C, while night torpor bouts last several hours and can reduce body temperature by up to 10 °C.<ref name=Kaplan/>

==Conservation and threats==
[[File:Podargus strigoides -West Ryde, Sydney, New South Wales-8.jpg|thumb|right|Perching on a balcony in Sydney, Australia]]

The conservation status of tawny frogmouths is "[[least concern]]" due to their widespread distribution.<ref name=Billabong>{{cite web|title=Tawny Frogmouth|url=http://billabongsanctuary.com.au/native-animals/birds/tawny-frogmouth|publisher=Billabong Sanctuary|access-date=20 May 2014}}</ref> A number of ongoing threats to the health of the population, though, are known. Many bird and mammalian carnivores are known to prey upon the tawny frogmouth.<ref name=Geiser/> Native birds, including [[Australian raven|ravens]], [[butcherbird]]s, and [[currawong]]s, may steal the protein-rich eggs to feed their own young.<ref name="Kaplan"/> Birds of prey such as [[hobby (bird)|hobbies]] and [[falcon]]s, as well as [[rodent]]s and tree-climbing [[snake]]s, also cause major damage to the clutches by taking eggs and nestlings. In subtropical areas where food is available throughout the year, tawny frogmouths sometimes start brooding earlier in winter to avoid the awakening of snakes after brumation. Since 1998, a cluster of cases of [[neurological]] disease has occurred in tawny frogmouths in the Sydney area, caused by the parasite ''[[Angiostrongylus cantonensis]]'', a rat [[lungworm]].<ref name=Ma>{{cite journal|last=Ma|first=G.|title=Tawny Frogmouths and Brushtail Possums as sentinels for ''Angriostrongylus cantonensis'', the rat lungworm|journal=Veterinary Parasitology|date=2013|volume=192|issue=1–3|pages=158–165|doi=10.1016/j.vetpar.2012.11.009|pmid=23218219|display-authors=etal}}</ref>

===Human impact===
Tawny frogmouths face a number of threats from human activities and pets. They are often killed or injured on [[rural]] roads during feeding, as they fly in front of cars when chasing insects illuminated in the beam of the headlights.<ref name=BackyardBuddies/> Large-scale [[land clearing]] of [[eucalypt]] trees and intense [[Bushfires in Australia|bushfires]] are serious threats to their populations, as they tend not to move to other areas if their homes are destroyed.<ref name=SeaWorld/> House cats are the most significant introduced predator of the tawny frogmouth, but dogs and [[fox]]es are known to also occasionally kill the birds.<ref name=Kaplan/> When tawny frogmouths pounce to catch prey on the ground, they are slow to return to flight and vulnerable to attack from these predators.<ref name=Billabong/>

As they have adapted to live in close proximity to human populations, tawny frogmouths are at high risk of exposure to [[pesticide]]s.<ref name=SeaWorld>{{cite web|title=Tawny Frogmouth|url=http://seaworld.org/animal-info/animal-bytes/birds/tawny-frogmouth|publisher=SeaWorld Parks & Entertainment|access-date=20 May 2014}}</ref> Continued widespread use of [[insecticide]]s and rodent poisons are hazardous, as they remain in the system of the target animal and can be fatal to a tawny frogmouth that eats them.<ref name=Billabong/> The effect of these toxins is often indirect, as they can be absorbed into fatty tissue with the bird experiencing no overt signs of ill health until the winter, when the fat deposits are drawn on and the poison enters the bloodstream.<ref name=Kaplan/>
